---
title : |
    Responses to Reviewers: 
    "Gender in Teaching: Insights from Five Million Syllabi on Collaboration, Interdisciplinarity, and Reading Selections"
subtitle: |
    Manuscript ID: PNASNEXUS-2025-00959-T
authors: 
    - Andrea Blasco
    - Elisa Orlandi

mainfont: Arial
fontsize: 12pt
linestretch: 1.5
geometry: "margin=0.5in"
bibliography: [../refs.bib]
--- 

Dear Editor and Reviewers, 

We thank you for the constructive and thoughtful feedback on our manuscript. We have revised the paper substantially to address all comments. Below, we respond point by point. Reviewer comments are in italics; our responses follow. Page and line references refer to the clean revised manuscript. 

# Editor Comments
 
**Thank you for considering PNAS Nexus as an outlet for your work. We have solicited and now received two in-depth reviews of your paper.**

**Both of the two reviewers found the overall quality of this paper was appreciable. Yet, in order to meet the criteria of publishing at PNAS Nexus, several critical revisions are needed. Given that the issues raised by the review team are rather serious, an ultimate acceptance cannot be guaranteed, though I do encourage you to submit a revised version to address all the questions raised by the reviewers. I summarize the core issues below.**

**First, the review team suggested you reorganized the introduction of the manuscript so as to further clarify the major focus of your research.**

We substantially rewrote the Introduction and literature review to clarify the central research question and contributions. As suggested by Reviewer 1, the revised text now includes a new Literature section with separate subsections each discussing separate strands of literature particularly relevant to our study: (i) research on how gender influences collaboration patterns, (ii) the representation of women in course materials, (iii) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams, and (iv) the propensity of such teams to innovate. We believe this new structure contributes to clarify our contribution and the relevant litereatute. 


**Second, the review team raised questions the different findings from prior research (e.g., Dion et al., 2018) and you may also need to address the issue regarding whether teachers can have freedom to choose whether to engage in team teaching, the incentives, and how all these may affect your findings.**

We added a paragraph comparing our findings to Dion et al. (2018) and related studies both in the Literature and in the Discussion section. We also expanded the discussion of institutional variation in team teaching, acknowledging that instructors’ ability to co-teach depends on administrative structures and credit systems that vary across institutions and countries.

Changes: Literature/Background [pp. X-Y, lines A-B]; Discussion [pp. X-Y, lines C-D].

**Third, research methodology and interpretation of the results. Please follow the suggestions from the review team to address issues related to research methodology, including but not limited to use of controls, lack of representativeness of your data coverage, missing data, and measures (e.g., novelty). Please also pay attention to presentation and interpretation of your findings (e.g., temporal changes seen in the graphs). Would you be able to get more data and see the influence of COVID 19 on the findings? The review team also requested you run additional analyses (e.g., US versus non-US and by subject areas such as natural sciences, humanities, social sciences).**



* Controls: As requested by Reviewer 1, we added course-level indicators for basic, advanced, graduate and a residual "unknown" category, along with our other controls in the regressions. The main results of our analysis remain the same.  

* Representativeness: We have clarified that our dataset includes 50% of ROR education organizations. Additionally, we conducted a detailed analysis of the representativeness of gender distribution. First, we show the correlation between the women-to-men ratio and the distribution of academic staff across countries. Next, we examine the gender distribution among graduates by field of education. Overall, our findings indicate a reasonable level of representativeness. However, because no comprehensive census of syllabi exists, we cannot fully verify completeness at the institutional level. We acknowledge this limitation in the Discussion section.

* Instructor gender identification: We have included a subsection in the Supporting Inoformation that outlines the gender disambiguiation process. We note that the process follows state-of-the-art methods (e.g., genderise.io) that have been employed in other dataset, including Dion et al. xxxx with high accuracy. We also clarify that approximately 12% of instructors are classified as “unknown” due to the use of initials or abbreviated names (e.g., J. instead of John or Jane). 

* Measures: We have expaned the measures of novelty to better capture the multidimensional definition. We renamed the age of readings as "recenecy" as suggested by the reviewe rbut we also introduce two new measures (1) a measure of conventionality of readings, captured by the number of citations of a reading in the other syllabi published before; and (2) a maesure of the aerage scientific onvelty of the paper compuated as the average Uzzi et al. atypicality measure extracted from SciSciNet.  Overall, we believe the three measures provide a comprehnsive view of the novelty of the courses. 

* Additional analyses: We ran separate models for U.S. vs. non-U.S. institutions and by broad disciplinary area, STEM vs non-STEM. The main conclusions remain consistent, although we find that the effect size varies across STEM vs non-STEM. 

* Temporal patterns: We extended the discussion of time trends, highlighting the average in the plots with smooth trends that account for it.

* Presentation: Reference groups are now standardized across all figures and specified explicitly in each caption. 

* COVID-19. 

* Robustness: All robustness analyses are included in the Supplementary Appendix (Tables S1-S3). 

Changes: Methods and Supplement [pp. X-Y; Tables S1-S3]; Discussion [pp. X-Y]. 

\clearpage

# Responses to Reviewers

## Reviewer 1 

**The paper examines an interesting and important topic: how the composition of teaching teams in higher education matters for three pedagogical outcomes - the age of readings assigned, the gender of the authors of the readings assigned, and the interdisciplinarity of the course. This is an area lacking systematic quantitative analysis, and I believe that if the analysis and results become more improved and robust, it could make a contribution to the literature. However, a few problems exist that require revision.**

**The literature review needs to be re-organized into three subsections that clearly delineate the three arguments that the paper wants to make. * first, on "how courses taught by FEMALE instructors may contain more female authored readings".    * second, on "why MIXED-gender team would assign more novel readings".   * third, on "how MIXED-gender teams can increase interdisciplinary teaching"   * Currently, the structure looks very hidden to me, especially given that sometimes the authors discuss the effect of "female" while other times they discuss the effect of "mixed gender".**


We thank the reviewer for this helpful comment. We agree that the original literature review did not sufficiently highlight the conceptual foundation of our analysis. In response, we have restructured the literature into a new “Literature” section with clearly delineated subsections. Although the reviewer suggested a three-part organization, we separated gendered collaboration patterns from gendered course content to enhance conceptual clarity. The introductory paragraph to the revised literature review now reads:

> Research shows that gender and institutional structures shape key aspects of academic work, from team formation to curriculum design. Four strands of this literature are particularly relevant to our study: (i) research on how gender influences collaboration patterns, (ii) the representation of women in course materials, (iii) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams, and (iv) the propensity of such teams to innovate. These insights provide the conceptual foundation for our study, which we review in detail below.

In addition, following Reviewer 2’s request to expand our analysis to the pre- and post-Covid-19 periods, we now include a fifth subsection summarizing the literature on how Covid-19 has affected academic collaboration. This addition further clarifies the context for our extended empirical analysis.

**There seems to be no control for the level of courses (ie., 101, 200+, 300+ and so on based on course code), but that could greatly influence the level of interdisciplinarity, age of course readings, or who teaches it. This is totally doable with Open Syllabus data to my knowledge**

We thank the reviewer for this valuable suggestion. While course level is not explicitly provided in the Open Syllabus data, it can be inferred from course codes (e.g., “Psychology 101” typically indicating an introductory course, and “Psychology 401” an advanced one). Following @biasi2022education, we classified courses into four levels based on their codes (basic, advanced, graduate or unknown); their validation suggests this approach achieves roughly 85% accuracy in the U.S. context. Although no equivalent validation exists for European institutions, and accuracy may therefore be lower, we additionally used the number of assigned readings in each syllabus as a further proxy for course level. Our results remain robust when incorporating these measures.

In the revised manuscript:

**Methods**: p. 9, we included: 

> Because course level (e.g., undergraduate or graduate) was not explicitly reported in the data, we inferred it from course codes. For example, “Psychology 101” typically denotes an introductory course, whereas “Psychology 401” indicates a more advanced offering. Using this heuristic, we categorized syllabi into four levels: basic, advanced, graduate, or unknown (see Section \@ref(course-level) for the details).^[
  According to @biasi2022education, this classification procedure is approximately 85% accurate for a sample of U.S. courses that could be matched to catalogues with verifiable information.
]

**Results**: All the regression results reported in figures 7 to 12 include course level controls. 

**Supporting Information**: We included a new subsection (p. 45) that reads:

> To determine the level of each course listed in the syllabi, we first extracted the course codes for each syllabus (e.g., Psychology 101, Econ101). Then, we standardised all course codes by converting them to uppercase and removing any non-alphanumeric characters. From each cleaned code, we extracted the first sequence of digits and used the leading digit of this number as an indicator of course level. Codes beginning with 1 or 2 were classified as *basic*, those beginning with 3 or 4 as *advanced*, and those beginning with 5 or higher as *graduate*. Codes that did not contain a recognisable numeric component were assigned an *unknown* level. This approach enabled consistent categorisation across heterogeneous code formats. Furthermore, on a subset of 56,000 U.S. syllabi, prior work [@biasi2022education] showed that the course levels obtained using this procedure achieve an accuracy of 85% when compared with the official course levels listed in the corresponding catalog. This provides confidence in the quality of the classification for the U.S. context. For EU countries, no comparable benchmark currently exists. 



**Although it is understandable to not have perfectly representative coverage of fields and schools given the scraped nature of this online based dataset -- this lack of representativeness is indeed heavily related to the aggregated estimates that the authors presented in the graphs throughout the results section. For example, when estimating whether mixed gender teaching groups have more interdisciplinarity than male-only groups, we need to know whether both types of teaching groups that are used in the analysis are proportional to the real distribution of the specific discipline in the specific institution and the specific country. Otherwise, how can we be sure that the aggregated / average estimates are not affected by the distribution of the cases selected in the operational data in this study?** 


We thank the reviewer for raising this important concern about representativeness and its implications for the aggregated estimates presented in the results. We agree that understanding how well our dataset reflects the underlying disciplinary, institutional, and national distributions is essential. In response, we have expanded both the Methods and Supporting Information to make the representativeness assessment more explicit and transparent. 

Specifically, we compared the proportions of women and men instructors in our dataset with official statistics on academic staff across countries. This analysis shows strong correlations for most countries, particularly English-speaking ones, where coverage of syllabi is highest. We also examined representativeness at the field level by correlating the gender distribution in our data with U.S. NCSES statistics on Ph.D. graduates by academic field. Here too we observe high correlations in many major fields. Taken together, these analyses indicate that the dataset is reasonably representative for the purposes of our study.

**Methods**: we included the following sentence:

> These figures broadly align with the 45% of female academic staff reported in OECD countries [@oecd2022]. Although official statistics to assess the representativeness of this gender population are not available, a detailed comparison with official statistics (\@ref(representativeness-of-the-gender-distribution)) shows that the observed trends in the proportions of women and men are highly correlated with representative statistics on academic staff across countries and with the distribution of graduates across fields (used as a proxy for the number of instructors). This analysis indicates a reasonable representativeness of our data.

**Supporting Information**: We added a dedicated subsection titled "Representativeness of the gender distribution": 

> One potential concern for our analysis is the representativeness of the data. Our dataset covers several million courses and thousands of educational institutions, corresponding to about 50% of the educational organisations in the ROR, which makes the results interesting in their own right. Nevertheless, one may question how representative these data are of the broader population of instructors. Although we cannot directly test representativeness, we evaluate it in two ways. First, we examine whether the overall female-to-male ratio over time in each country correlates with OECD statistics on academic staff by gender. Second, for the U.S., we assess whether the sex ratio by field and year correlates with NCSES data on Ph.D. graduates by academic field, country, and year, which serves as a proxy for the maximum number of employable instructors.

> As illustrated in Figure \@ref(fig:country-coverage), our analysis of overall trends reveals a very high correlation for English-speaking countries (U.S. = .98, U.K. = .90), and greater heterogeneity for non-English-speaking countries, where representativeness is expected to be weaker given our focus on English-language syllabi. The analysis based on Ph.D. graduates similarly shows strong field-level correlations in many fields (Figure \@ref(fig:field-coverage-us)), such as in Natural Sciences (rho = .91), Social sciences and journalism (rho = .90), Education (rho = .77). However, there are fields with considerably lower correlation, such as in Health and welfare (rho = .14) or IT (rho = .29). This heterogeneity may stem from differences across disciplines in the proportion of Ph.D. graduates who remain in academia (e.g., biology has a comparatively high share of graduates who pursue careers outside academia). Nevertheless, these patterns suggest that, at least for English-speaking countries and in many academic fields, our data are reasonably representative.


**Genderizer: 12% of the syllabi were reported to have unknown gender (p 5). Is this a problem of missing, or is it a problem of the genderizer being unable to estimate the gender (many asian names are gender neutral) -- how is this going to affect the estimates?**

We thank the reviewer for the opportunity to clarify this point. In the Supporting Information, we have included a section on gender that describes the process of gender disambiguation. In brief, OpenSyllabus uses a version of Genderize.io, which has an estimated overall accuracy of 98% in academic publications. If the probability of an author being female exceeds 0.5, the author is classified as female; otherwise, they are classified as male. However, not all instructors listed in the syllabi provide their full first names; some list only initials (e.g., "J." for "John" or "Jane"). In such cases, OpenSyllabus assigns the author to the "unknown" category.

> Changes: Supporting Information [pp. XX]. 

**novelty = using more recent readings in the syllabus: a decision the authors tried to defend on page 6. However, I do not really buy it. It is NOT a conventional construction of novelty simply by using the age of readings (especially given that the authors are aware that STS literature developed much better measurements of it) -- for example, old readings can also be rediscovered as highly novel in new contexts where they regain relevance and attention that they did not have before.** 

Thank you for this insightful comment. We agree with your point and have revised the manuscript accordingly.
 
First, we now clarify that measuring “novelty” in the context of syllabi is inherently challenging. Syllabi often cite only a few textbooks, making it difficult to compute standard novelty metrics, such as atypicality (based on the network of journal publications). Moreover, novelty measured at the time of publication may not reflect the novelty students experience at the time of teaching; i.e., a paper that was highly novel in year x may be perceived as well-established or even dated when incorporated into a later syllabus.
 
Second, your comment helped us recognise that teaching novelty is multidimensional. An instructor may rely on older material but use it in pedagogically innovative ways (“teaching innovation”). Alternatively, they may assign papers at the frontier of scientific knowledge (“scientific novelty”). Or they may introduce newer readings (“recency”).
 
In response, we have revised our measurement strategy to address these distinctions:
 
1. We no longer label the age of readings as “novelty.” Instead, we now explicitly refer to this dimension as the recency of assigned materials.
2. We introduce two new novelty measures to more appropriately capture the multidimensional nature of teaching innovation:
    - Teaching novelty: the number of times the same resource appears in other syllabi. Rarely used readings indicate greater pedagogical novelty.
    - Scientific novelty: the atypicality of papers at the time they were taught, which we compute by matching the readings DOIs to SciCite (a large dataset of pre-computed measures of novelty).
 
These changes improve conceptual clarity and allow us to more accurately capture different forms of novelty relevant to teaching
 

Changes: Methods [pp. X-Y]. 

**On page 10, I found myself wondering why the figures tend to have different reference groups every now and then. For example, in section 3.1.3, the authors wrote, "between 2000 and 2019 courses taught by a single female instructor ... ...than otherwise similar courses taught by **a single man** in that year... ... Similarly, courses taught by two women also assign newer materials than those taught by **two men** " -- this seems to me that the reference group in the first comparison is "courses taught by single man" while the reference group for the second is "courses taught by two men".  This is highly confusing because in the note of the specific figure, it says the reference is "courses taught by single male" . If different reference groups are used, then it must be clarified early, and in the figures' notes as well**
 

Response: We standardized reference categories across all models and clearly report them in figure captions (e.g., “single male instructor” as the baseline). 

Changes: Figures [F1-Fx]; caption notes updated. 


\clearpage


## Reviewer 2

**In this study, the authors analyze 2.9 million syllabi across 15 countries from 1990 to 2019 to assess how the instructors’ sex (individually or in teams) influences the degree of interdisciplinarity, the average age of assigned readings, and the percentage of female authors included in the syllabus. A linear mixed effects model shows that female teams and mixed gender teams assign more work by women scholars and that the interdisciplinarity range is higher in these instances. Female instructors/teams tend to assign newer work as well. The study leverages an interesting dataset and uses a clever research design including 1) text analysis (bag of words) to capture a syllabus’ level of interdisciplinarity and 2) a Monte Carlo randomization approach to assess actual gender distributions of teaching teams and what is expected based on institutional data. The study also provides insights into how team-teaching influences syllabus content, especially in terms of the sex of the teams (MM, MF, FF). The findings will be of interest to readers of the journal.**

**The finding that female teaching teams tend to include more readings by female authors accords with findings from the citation literature. See for example: Dion, Michelle L., Jane Lawrence Sumner, and Sara McLaughlin Mitchell. 2018. “Gendered citation patterns across political science and social science methodology fields.” Political Analysis 26(3): 312-327. On the other hand, Dion et al do not find that mixed gender teams cite more work by women in research articles than male teams, so there is an interesting bit of difference in these results.**

Thank you for the reference. We have now highlighted it into the literature review and discussed its relevance in the Discussion section. That is (p. XX): 

> *These findings align with prior research on gendered citation patterns in the academic literature (e.g., Dion, Sumner, & Mitchell, 2018), which shows that female scholars are more likely to cite work by other women. Interestingly, Dion et al. find that mixed-gender research teams do not cite more women than male-only teams, highlighting a divergence from our course-level results. Taken together, our analysis suggests that instructor gender shapes reading lists in ways that may differ from research citation patterns and that mixed-gender teaching teams could foster greater gender diversity in assigned readings.*

**There are a lot of temporal changes seen in the graphs and I'm wondering what is driving them. In many cases, the expansion towards more mixed/female team teaching seems to experience a backsliding in the last few years of the data. I think the authors should try to identify what may be driving these changes. It’s unfortunate that the data end in 2019 because it would be interesting to see how the Covid-19 pandemic altered these patterns further.**

We have analyzed the temporal patterns in detail. First, the trends in team compositions reflect broadly the increasing presence of women in the academic profession over the period 2000–2019. Second, while there are some year-to-year fluctuations in the associations between team composition and outcomes, these differences are rarely statistically significant and appear largely attributable to noise. However, to better highlight systematic temporal patterns, we have revised our plots to include a smoothed trend that averages the estimated effects over time using a generalized additive model (GAM) with cubic regression splines and shrinkage, weighting years according to estimation accuracy. This approach reveals an overall [decreasing effect of x] over time, which we now discuss in the revised Discussion section.

**There’s almost an assumption that faculty can engage in team teaching if they desire, but that is not true in many disciplines or institutions. This possibility is briefly mentioned on page 11, but I think you should discuss this more. In my institution, it would be difficult to get credit for team teaching. I teach 4 courses per year and typically any team teaching would be done as an overload beyond the 4 courses. I realize your fixed effects for institutions and countries help to pick that up, but I'm curious what the data looks like descriptively across countries.**

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point and share the same concern. Unfortunately, we cannot test this empirically with our current data. Instead, we have added a discussion of this issue in the Discussion section (p. XX):

> *A further limitation is that institutional policies regarding teaching loads and the recognition of co-teaching vary substantially across countries and institutions. In many universities, team teaching does not fully count toward teaching loads or is treated as an overload, limiting faculty opportunities to engage in it even if they wish to. Moreover, teaching teams are not always determined by individual instructors’ preferences: in many institutions, course assignments and team composition are partially or entirely determined at the departmental or program level—for example, by course coordinators, curriculum structures, or administrative rules. In such cases, team formation may reflect institutional or organizational preferences rather than instructors’ choices. Our data do not capture these institutional constraints or assignment mechanisms, which may mechanically reduce or shape the observed incidence of mixed-gender teams. Consequently, our descriptive patterns reflect both individual preferences and the structural opportunities and assignment practices provided by institutions.*


**Similarly, the data is very U.S. dominant (66% of all syllabi). What if you run a model for U.S. institutions and compare that to a model with institutions in non-U.S. countries? Are the results similar or different? Why is team teaching less common in EU countries? This is the kind of thing I wanted more discussion of and some analyses to explore these differences.**

We appreciate this comment. As noted, we already discuss cross-country differences in team formation. Following your suggestion, we expanded this analysis to examine potential heterogeneity in the association between team composition and course readings across countries. Specifically, we compared the United States to all non-U.S. institutions with respect to (1) novelty, (2) female-authored readings, and (3) interdisciplinarity. The results reveal no systematic deviations across these outcomes: the estimates for U.S. institutions closely track those obtained for the full sample, suggesting that the main patterns we document are robust across national contexts. We now report these findings in the Supporting Information (Figure SI-4, p. 51).

Hence, we clarified the trends by countries, showing that differences in teams. Although we lack data on college professors collaborations, the TALIS on secondary and primary do not show primarly driven by differences in Traditional teaching culture among teachers. Others reasons should be more on other lack of institutional support or other barriers [?]. 

**It could be interesting to run separate analyses in general areas as well (e.g., sciences, humanities, social sciences) to see if the general patterns generalize across these areas.**

Many thanks for this helpful suggestion. In response, we conducted additional heterogeneity analysis across major disciplinary areas. Specifically, we compared STEM against non-STEM fields. This exercise revealed several noteworthy differences:  while the direction of the associations is consistent across fields, the magnitude of the effects varies. We now report these results in a new subsection on Heterogeneity (p. 20). The new paragraph on STEM fields reads:

> *Figure 12 highlights several key differences between STEM and non-STEM fields, based on separate models estimated for STEM fields compared against results from the full sample. The gap in female-author citations associated with women instructors remains consistent but is significantly smaller in STEM fields, roughly half the size observed in all fields. The positive association between mixed-gender teams and conventional readings is larger in STEM fields. Conversely, the gap in atypicality widens for women teaching alone, although the effect has been diminishing over time. In contrast, the gap in the age of assigned readings between women and men teaching alone is smaller in STEM. With respect to interdisciplinarity, the estimated differences are small and not consistently significant across years. Together, these results provide strong evidence of heterogeneity between STEM and non-STEM fields in the strength of the associations between team composition and course materials.*

# References
